phrasal_verbs.meaning: become friends again after an argument
look after a child until he or she becomes an adult
fall in love with; believe (a lie/trick/etc)
keep treating someone badly or unfairly
have an argument with and stop being friends
become calm after being upset, etc; stay in one place or get married and live quietly
support in an argument or fight
think that you are better than

questions.sentence: The whole team said they had ...... themselves at the company outing last Friday.
Tom always hangs out with a large ...... of friends from his university days.
Children often become very ...... when they have to wait a long time for something.
Many people these days are living into ...... age, which puts pressure on healthcare systems.
The new teacher seems to be very ...... with the students in her class.
She grew up in a small town and led a perfectly ...... life until she became famous.
When I lost my job, my neighbour was very ...... and spent hours listening to my worries.
James is very ...... and takes every small comment personally.
Security had to intervene when the ...... of protesters started pushing against the barriers.
My uncle claims he once worked with that actor when the man was still .......
It's hard to **assist / support** a large household on just one income these days.
Arriving late without an apology is so **usual / typical** of my brother.
Since moving abroad, she doesn't have any **close / near** relatives she can visit easily.
In **ancient / old** civilisations, people often believed in many different gods.
Nobody wanted to be at **blame / fault** for the confusion during the meeting.
It is considered very **kind / polite** to hold the door open for the person behind you.
Her parents were absolutely **enjoyed / pleased** when she told them she had passed her exams.
He has a very strong **connection / relationship** with his older sister, and they talk every day.

C.title: Phrasal verbs (gap fill)
C.instructions: Complete using the words from the box. You need to use one word twice.

questions.sentence: Some of us accept everyone around us, while others seem to (1) ...... down on people who are different from them.
Some of us hold a grudge for years, while others (2) ...... up and forgive very easily.
As we (3) ...... up, we discover more about ourselves and find that we (4) ...... on better with some people than others.
Our values and habits are strongly influenced by the people who (5) ...... us up during childhood.
If we (6) ...... up to certain people in our lives, we often try to imitate their behaviour.
But if someone constantly (7) ...... us down and we (8) ...... out with them repeatedly, we are likely to drift apart.

D.title: Phrasal verbs (transformation)
D.instructions: Complete the sentences using a phrasal verb from the box. Use the correct form.

questions.sentence: The elderly gentleman ...... quietly in hospital, surrounded by his family.
No matter what happens, I always ...... my sister when she is being treated unfairly.
Everyone at school could see that Daniel had completely ...... Sofia.
That older student ...... the younger kids every time they walk into the classroom.
My manager was completely ...... when she heard how quickly I had finished the task.
After years of travelling the world, Paulo eventually met someone special and ...... in Portugal.
I bumped into your mum at the market yesterday and she ...... you.

E.title: Phrases and collocations (gap fill)
E.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

questions.sentence: Would you ...... me a favour and remind Jake about the meeting at two?
They got married young and decided to ...... a family straight away.
The kind stranger ...... pity on the lost child and helped him find his parents.
I really wanted to say something, but I didn't ...... the courage to speak up.
My uncle absolutely hates ...... an argument and always has to raise his voice.
The committee's new proposal didn't ...... with the public's approval at all.
Could you ...... care of feeding the cat while we're away on holiday?
It's very easy to ...... in love with a place when you travel somewhere truly beautiful.
She finds it quite hard to ...... friends because she tends to be quite reserved.
The spy was travelling ...... disguise and nobody at the border recognised him.
You seem to be in a very good ......! Did something nice happen today?
If you keep ...... your promises, people will eventually stop trusting you.
...... a really strange dream last night in which all my colleagues were speaking in French.
The charity is trying to convince people ...... the importance of regular volunteering.
Many artists feel they need to be completely independent ...... commercial pressures.
Local residents strongly object ...... the new motorway being built through their village.

Most students seem to agree ...... the professor's interpretation of the results.

The CEO was publicly attacked ...... company resources on unnecessary travel.

G.title: Error correction (extra words)
G.instructions: Find the extra word in each line. Click it.

questions.sentence: Sharing a world with others means accepting differences, and whether you like with or not, every community has its own rules and values.
We may not always approve of that our neighbours' habits, but we usually have no choice but to coexist.
It is nearly impossible to force other people in to adopt your lifestyle or your values.
Society bans from certain kinds of extreme behaviour, and if you can persuade to enough people, you might even change the law.
For the most part, though, you have to let off people make their own choices, just as you expect them to respect yours.
You don't have to pretend it that you agree with everything around you, but accepting others is often the only realistic option.

H.title: Word formation (text)
H.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line.

questions.sentence: I had a really silly (1) ...... (ARGUE) with my flatmate last week over something completely trivial.
It all started when we were discussing (2) ...... (MARRY) and whether it was still important in modern society.
I argued that (3) ...... (POLITE) matters a great deal in any close relationship, but she insisted that (4) ...... (KIND) is far more significant.
In the end, we were (5) ...... (ABLE) to find any common ground, and she went to bed without saying goodnight.
I really hope this doesn't damage our (6) ...... (FRIEND).

I.title: Word formation (sentences)
I.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

questions.sentence: My new colleague has an incredibly positive ...... (PERSON) and everyone in the office gets on well with her.
She has a very open and honest ...... (RELATE) with her parents and can talk to them about anything.
The museum has just released a collection of the artist's private ...... (CORRESPOND), which is truly fascinating.
He seemed ...... (WILLING) to discuss what had happened, but I eventually managed to get the full story.
I bit my nails ...... (NERVE) as I waited outside the interview room.
Pay no attention to what Daniel says about you. It's nothing but ...... (JEALOUS).
In previous generations, children were expected to be completely ...... (OBEY) and never to challenge their parents.
She blames her ...... (HAPPY) on her lack of close friends, but I think the problem runs deeper.
Being selected as team captain was, for him, a remarkable personal ...... (ACHIEVE).
She is so sensitive that you really have to be very ...... (CARE) about the words you choose around her.
